To derive the total query response time function $ T_{\text{Total}}(Q) $ we must consider the individual response times of each database and sum them based on the proportion of queries each handles. 

The query response time for each database is defined as a function of the number of queries it processes. 

The total number of queries is $ Q $ and the proportion of queries handled by each database is given as $ \alpha Q $ for MySQL $ \beta Q $ for Oracle and $ (1-\alpha-\beta)Q $ for Sybase. 

The total query response time is the sum of the response times for each database multiplied by their respective query counts. 

The response time for MySQL is calculated using the function $ T_{\text{MySQL}}(Q) = a \cdot Q^2 + b \cdot e^{cQ} $ and it is applied to the number of queries it handles $ \alpha Q $. 

The response time for Oracle is calculated using the function $ T_{\text{Oracle}}(Q) = d \cdot \ln(Q+1) + \frac{e}{Q+1} $ and it is applied to the number of queries it handles $ \beta Q $. 

The response time for Sybase is calculated using the function $ T_{\text{Sybase}}(Q) = f \cdot Q^{\frac{3}{2}} - g \cdot \sqrt{Q} $ and it is applied to the number of queries it handles $ (1-\alpha-\beta)Q $. 

The total query response time is the sum of the response times for all three databases. 

The total query response time function $ T_{\text{Total}}(Q) $ is derived by summing the individual response times. 

The function is expressed as the sum of the response times for each database multiplied by the number of queries each handles. 

The derived function for the total query response time is the sum of the individual response times for each database.
